name: Auth0 Misconfigured CORS Leaking JWTs
description: Web application with misconfigured CORS on the Auth0 callback endpoint allows cross-origin requests from attacker-controlled domains, enabling JWT token exfiltration from authenticated sessions.
mitigations: Restrict allowed origins to known application domains only
Validate the state parameter on every OAuth callback
Set short JWT expiry (15 minutes) with refresh token rotation
Monitor Auth0 logs for unexpected origin patterns

name: Cognito User Pool Enumeration via Error Differentiation
description: Unauthenticated attacker sending crafted requests to Cognito login endpoints can enumerate valid usernames by observing different error messages for existing vs. non-existing accounts.
mitigations: Enable Cognito's 'Prevent user existence errors' option
Return generic error messages regardless of account existence
Implement rate limiting and account lockout policies
Use email-only login to reduce enumerable surface

name: Firebase Anonymous Auth Privilege Escalation
description: Attacker exploiting Firebase anonymous authentication obtains a valid UID and can access Firestore documents whose security rules check only for authentication presence, not identity or role.
mitigations: Write Firestore security rules that check resource ownership, not just auth status
Disable anonymous sign-in unless explicitly required by the feature
Separate anonymous user quotas from authenticated user quotas
Audit security rules with the Firebase Rules Simulator regularly

name: Supabase Row Level Security Policy Bypass
description: Developer disabling or incorrectly configuring Supabase Row Level Security (RLS) on a public table allows any authenticated user to read, update, or delete rows belonging to other users.
mitigations: Enable RLS on every user-facing table — verify with FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY
Test RLS policies with both anon and authenticated roles before deployment
Use Supabase's Policy Editor to audit effective policies per table
Integrate automated RLS tests in CI using the Supabase CLI

name: OAuth Authorization Code Interception via Open Redirector
description: Attacker exploiting an open redirector in the application's OAuth redirect_uri parameter intercepts the authorization code before exchange, gaining access to the victim's account.
mitigations: Validate redirect_uri against a strict allowlist — no wildcards or open redirectors
Implement PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for all public clients
Bind authorization codes to the client_id and redirect_uri at issue time
Set authorization code TTL to 60 seconds or less

name: Auth0 JWT Audience Claim Not Validated
description: Backend service that skips JWT audience (aud) validation accepts tokens issued for other Auth0 applications, allowing lateral movement between services sharing the same identity provider.
mitigations: Validate the 'aud' claim matches the API identifier on every token verification
Use per-service API identifiers in Auth0, not a shared identifier
Fail closed on missing or unrecognized audience claims
Automate token validation with Auth0's official SDK

name: Clerk Webhook Signature Bypass via Replay
description: Attacker capturing a valid Clerk webhook with its signature replays the same event multiple times, triggering duplicate user provisioning, role grants, or billing credits if the receiver lacks idempotency checks.
mitigations: Validate Clerk webhook signatures using svix or the Clerk SDK on every request
Reject webhooks with timestamps older than 5 minutes (included in Clerk headers)
Store and check webhook event IDs to enforce idempotency
Return 200 quickly and process events asynchronously to avoid double delivery

name: Magic Link One-Time Token Reuse
description: Email-based magic link implementation that fails to invalidate the token after first use allows an attacker who intercepts the link (via email forwarding, log leakage, or referrer headers) to authenticate as the victim.
mitigations: Invalidate magic link tokens immediately after first use
Set token TTL to 10-15 minutes maximum
Bind the token to the requesting browser's IP or fingerprint
Send email only over TLS and warn users to keep links private

name: SSO SAML Assertion Signature Wrapping Attack
description: Attacker exploiting a SAML XML Signature Wrapping vulnerability crafts a SAML response that passes signature verification but contains a different assertion body, impersonating any user in the identity provider.
mitigations: Use a well-maintained SAML library — avoid implementing SAML parsing manually
Validate the signed element's ID matches what the service provider expects
Enable strict schema validation on incoming SAML responses
Pin the IdP certificate and alert on certificate changes

name: Cognito JWT Algorithm Confusion
description: Backend accepting Cognito JWTs without explicitly requiring RS256 algorithm can be exploited with algorithm confusion attacks (e.g., HS256 with the public key as the secret), forging valid tokens.
mitigations: Explicitly specify RS256 (or the configured algorithm) in the JWT library — never use 'auto'
Verify tokens using Cognito's JWKS endpoint with key pinning
Reject tokens with unexpected algorithm headers before signature verification
Use Cognito's own SDK for token verification rather than generic JWT libraries

name: Firebase Firestore Rules Missing Field-Level Restriction
description: Firestore security rules granting document-level read access without restricting which fields are exposed allow authenticated users to read sensitive fields (PII, payment info, internal flags) co-located in the same document.
mitigations: Use Firestore's field-level security rules (resource.data.sensitiveField) to restrict access
Store sensitive data in a separate sub-collection with tighter rules
Audit document schema to ensure sensitive fields are not co-located with public data
Use Cloud Functions as a backend layer to filter fields before returning to clients

name: Supabase Service Role Key Exposed in Client Code
description: Developer embedding the Supabase service role key (which bypasses RLS entirely) in client-side JavaScript or a public repository grants any user superuser-level database access.
mitigations: Use only the anon key in client-side code — service role key belongs server-side only
Rotate service role keys immediately if exposure is suspected
Add Supabase key patterns to pre-commit secret scanning hooks
Use environment variables in server deployments, never hardcode in source
